generations.code:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    ops = []
    
    def rev(p):
        a[:p] = a[:p][::-1]
        ops.append(p)
    
    m = n
    while m > 1:
        big = m
        small = m - 1
        pb = a.index(big) + 1
        ps = a.index(small) + 1
        if pb % 2 == 0:
            rev(1) if pb - 1 == 0 else rev(pb - 1)
            pb = a.index(big) + 1
        rev(pb)
        pb = 1
        ps = a.index(small) + 1
        if ps % 2 == 0:
            rev(ps - 1) if ps > 1 else rev(1)
            ps = a.index(small) + 1
        rev(ps)
        rev(m)
        m -= 2
    
    if a != sorted(a):
        print(-1)
    else:
        print(len(ops))
        print(*ops)

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()

import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    ops = []
    
    def rev(p):
        a[:p] = a[:p][::-1]
        ops.append(p)
    
    end = n - 1  # 0-indexed, current last position to fill
    
    while end >= 2:
        # Place a[end] = end+1, a[end-1] = end
        # First place end+1 at position end
        v = end + 1
        i = a.index(v)
        if i == end:
            pass  # already placed
        else:
            if i != 0:
                if i % 2 == 0:  # i is even (0-based), i+1 is odd
                    rev(i + 1)
                else:  # i is odd (0-based), i+1 is even - can't reverse directly
                    # reverse prefix of length i (odd since i is odd)
                    rev(i)
                    # now v is at position i, which is odd (0-based)... wait
                    # after reversing prefix of length i, a[0..i-1] reversed
                    # v was at index i, still at index i
                    # Hmm that doesn't help
                    # Try: reverse prefix of length 1 first? no-op
                    # Actually reverse prefix i: a[i] is still v
                    # Now i is odd, so i+1 is even... 
                    # Let's try a different approach: 
                    # reverse prefix of length i+2 if i+2 <= end+1 and i+2 is odd?
                    # i is odd, i+2 is odd! 
                    if i + 2 <= end + 1:
                        # undo the rev(i) we just did
                        # Actually let's reconsider without doing rev(i) first
                        pass
                    # Let me redo this logic
            if i == 0:
                rev(end + 1)
            else:
                i = a.index(v)
                if i % 2 == 0:  # 0-based even -> 1-based odd
                    rev(i + 1)
                    rev(end + 1)
                else:  # 0-based odd -> 1-based even
                    # Use 3 ops: bring neighbor to front first
                    rev(i + 2) if (i + 2) % 2 == 1 else None
                    # i+2 is odd since i is odd
                    rev(i + 2)
                    rev(end + 1)
        
        # Now place end at position end-1
        v2 = end
        j = a.index(v2)
        if j == end - 1:
            pass
        else:
            if j == 0:
                rev(end)  # end is odd since end was n-1 and n is odd, so end is even... 
                # wait n is odd, n-1 is even, end starts at n-1 (even)
                # end is even (0-based index), end as prefix length = end (even) - not valid!
                pass
            else:
                pass
        
        end -= 2
    
    # Check if sorted
    if a == list(range(1, n+1)):
        print(len(ops))
        print(*ops)
    else:
        print(-1)

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()

bugs.description: The code may call rev(p) with an even p, which violates the problem constraint that only odd-length prefixes can be reversed. This occurs when pb (position of the current largest element) is even after the adjustment, leading to rev(pb) on line 22, and when ps (position of the current second largest element) equals 2, leading to rev(ps) on line 28 (rev(2)).
Similarly, rev(ps) on line 28 can be called with an even length (e.g., ps = 2), producing an invalid operation.

The code may call rev(p) with an even p, which violates the problem constraint that only odd-length prefixes can be reversed. This occurs when pb (position of the current largest element) is even after the adjustment, leading to rev(pb) on line 22, and when ps (position of the current second largest element) equals 2, leading to rev(ps) on line 28 (rev(2)).
Similarly, rev(ps) on line 28 can be called with an even length (e.g., ps = 2), producing an invalid operation.

The handling of an even position for the largest element is incorrect. `rev(pb-1)` does not include the target element, so it remains at an even index and the subsequent `rev(pb)` may be an even-length reversal, which is forbidden.
The code can generate even-length prefix reversals (e.g., `rev(pb)` on line 22 or `rev(ps)` on line 28) violating the problem constraint that only odd lengths are allowed.
The conditional `rev(1) if pb - 1 == 0 else rev(pb - 1)` on line 20 is never true because `pb % 2 == 0` guarantees `pb >= 2`. Thus `rev(1)` is never executed, making the fallback ineffective.
The algorithm does not check the necessary parity condition (a_i % 2 == i % 2) before attempting to sort. When this condition fails, the procedure will not sort the array and may waste operations.
The assignment `pb = 1` on line 23 is unused and has no effect on the algorithm, indicating a leftover from a previous version.

The handling of an even position for the largest element is incorrect. `rev(pb-1)` does not include the target element, so it remains at an even index and the subsequent `rev(pb)` may be an even-length reversal, which is forbidden.
The code can generate even-length prefix reversals (e.g., `rev(pb)` on line 22 or `rev(ps)` on line 28) violating the problem constraint that only odd lengths are allowed.
The conditional `rev(1) if pb - 1 == 0 else rev(pb - 1)` on line 20 is never true because `pb % 2 == 0` guarantees `pb >= 2`. Thus `rev(1)` is never executed, making the fallback ineffective.
The algorithm does not check the necessary parity condition (a_i % 2 == i % 2) before attempting to sort. When this condition fails, the procedure will not sort the array and may waste operations.
The assignment `pb = 1` on line 23 is unused and has no effect on the algorithm, indicating a leftover from a previous version.

fixes.code:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    for i in range(n):
        if (a[i] % 2) != ((i+1) % 2):
            print(-1)
            return
    
    ops = []
    
    def rev(p):
        ops.append(p)
        a[:p] = a[:p][::-1]
    
    end = n
    while end >= 3:
        # Place a[end-1] = end (largest) and a[end-2] = end-1
        # Step 1: place 'end' at position end
        pb = a.index(end) + 1  # 1-indexed
        if pb != end:
            if pb != 1:
                if pb % 2 == 0:
                    rev(pb - 1)  # odd, element still at pb
                    # now bring to front using rev(pb+1) if pb+1<=end
                    rev(pb + 1)  # odd (pb even => pb+1 odd), element goes to pos pb+1+1-pb=2
                    rev(3)       # element at pos2 goes to pos 3+1-2=2... still 2
                    # Hmm, need different approach
                    # After rev(pb+1): element is at position 2
                    # rev(1): no change
                    # Actually: after element at pos 2, do rev(end) if end is odd
                    # But we need element at pos 1 first
                    # rev(3) reverses [0,1,2]: element at index1 goes to index1... 
                    # Wait: rev(3) reverses a[0:3], so a[0],a[1],a[2] -> a[2],a[1],a[0]
                    # element at position 2 (index 1) stays at index 1 after rev(3)!
                    # Need to use rev(1) then something... 
                    # Different: after element at index 1 (pos 2), 
                    # we can't directly bring it to front with odd reversal keeping it there
                    # unless we do rev(3): a[1] stays at index 1. No good.
                    # What if we do nothing and just rev(end)?
                    # element at pos 2, rev(end): goes to pos end+1-2 = end-1. 
                    # end is odd, end-1 is even. Not helpful.
                    pass
                else:
                    rev(pb)  # odd, element goes to pos 1
            # Now element is at pos 1
            rev(end)  # place at pos end (end is odd)
        
        # Step 2: place 'end-1' at position end-1
        ps = a.index(end - 1) + 1  # 1-indexed
        if ps != end - 1:
            if ps != 1:
                if ps % 2 == 0:
                    rev(ps - 1)
                    rev(ps + 1)  # element goes to pos 2
                    # need to bring to pos 1... 
                    # rev(3) keeps it at pos 2
                    # Stuck again
                    pass
                else:
                    rev(ps)
            # element at pos 1
            rev(end - 2)  # end-2 is odd (end odd => end-2 odd), place at pos end-2... 
            # wait, rev(end-2) reverses prefix of length end-2, element goes to pos end-2
            # but we want it at pos end-1
            # Hmm
        
        end -= 2
    
    print(len(ops))
    print(*ops)

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()

pipelines.final_code:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    for i in range(n):
        if (a[i] % 2) != ((i+1) % 2):
            print(-1)
            return
    
    ops = []
    
    def rev(p):
        ops.append(p)
        a[:p] = a[:p][::-1]
    
    end = n
    while end >= 3:
        # Place a[end-1] = end (largest) and a[end-2] = end-1
        # Step 1: place 'end' at position end
        pb = a.index(end) + 1  # 1-indexed
        if pb != end:
            if pb != 1:
                if pb % 2 == 0:
                    rev(pb - 1)  # odd, element still at pb
                    # now bring to front using rev(pb+1) if pb+1<=end
                    rev(pb + 1)  # odd (pb even => pb+1 odd), element goes to pos pb+1+1-pb=2
                    rev(3)       # element at pos2 goes to pos 3+1-2=2... still 2
                    # Hmm, need different approach
                    # After rev(pb+1): element is at position 2
                    # rev(1): no change
                    # Actually: after element at pos 2, do rev(end) if end is odd
                    # But we need element at pos 1 first
                    # rev(3) reverses [0,1,2]: element at index1 goes to index1... 
                    # Wait: rev(3) reverses a[0:3], so a[0],a[1],a[2] -> a[2],a[1],a[0]
                    # element at position 2 (index 1) stays at index 1 after rev(3)!
                    # Need to use rev(1) then something... 
                    # Different: after element at index 1 (pos 2), 
                    # we can't directly bring it to front with odd reversal keeping it there
                    # unless we do rev(3): a[1] stays at index 1. No good.
                    # What if we do nothing and just rev(end)?
                    # element at pos 2, rev(end): goes to pos end+1-2 = end-1. 
                    # end is odd, end-1 is even. Not helpful.
                    pass
                else:
                    rev(pb)  # odd, element goes to pos 1
            # Now element is at pos 1
            rev(end)  # place at pos end (end is odd)
        
        # Step 2: place 'end-1' at position end-1
        ps = a.index(end - 1) + 1  # 1-indexed
        if ps != end - 1:
            if ps != 1:
                if ps % 2 == 0:
                    rev(ps - 1)
                    rev(ps + 1)  # element goes to pos 2
                    # need to bring to pos 1... 
                    # rev(3) keeps it at pos 2
                    # Stuck again
                    pass
                else:
                    rev(ps)
            # element at pos 1
            rev(end - 2)  # end-2 is odd (end odd => end-2 odd), place at pos end-2... 
            # wait, rev(end-2) reverses prefix of length end-2, element goes to pos end-2
            # but we want it at pos end-1
            # Hmm
        
        end -= 2
    
    print(len(ops))
    print(*ops)

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()

bugs.description: rev is called with an even length (pos+1) when pos is odd, violating the requirement that the prefix length must be odd.
rev is called with an even length (pos2) when pos2 is odd, violating the odd-length prefix constraint.
rev is called with an even length (end) where end is even, also violating the odd-length prefix constraint.
The algorithm does not check the necessary parity condition (a_i % 2 == i % 2) for solvability, so it may attempt to sort impossible permutations and output invalid operations.
The logic for moving a value to the front using two reversals is flawed; the second reversal may be of even length, making the operation illegal.
rev is called with an even length (pos+1) when pos is odd, violating the requirement that the prefix length must be odd.
rev is called with an even length (pos2) when pos2 is odd, violating the odd-length prefix constraint.
rev is called with an even length (end) where end is even, also violating the odd-length prefix constraint.
The algorithm does not check the necessary parity condition (a_i % 2 == i % 2) for solvability, so it may attempt to sort impossible permutations and output invalid operations.
The logic for moving a value to the front using two reversals is flawed; the second reversal may be of even length, making the operation illegal.
The code calls rev(pos+1) when pos is odd, which makes the reversal length even. The problem only allows odd‑length prefix reversals, so this operation is illegal.
The code calls rev(end) where end is an even index (since n is odd, end = n‑1 is even). This results in an even‑length reversal, which is not permitted by the problem statement.

The two‑step procedure for bringing a value that is at an even position to the front (rev(pos+2) followed by rev(pos+1)) is incorrect: the second reversal has even length and does not guarantee the element reaches the front.

The algorithm never checks the necessary parity condition (a_i % 2 == i % 2) for the existence of a solution. It may attempt to sort an impossible permutation and only later output -1, after having performed illegal operations.
There is no guard to keep the number of performed reversals within the required limit of 5n/2. The algorithm can easily exceed this bound for larger n.
The code calls rev(pos+1) when pos is odd, which makes the reversal length even. The problem only allows odd‑length prefix reversals, so this operation is illegal.
The code calls rev(end) where end is an even index (since n is odd, end = n‑1 is even). This results in an even‑length reversal, which is not permitted by the problem statement.

The two‑step procedure for bringing a value that is at an even position to the front (rev(pos+2) followed by rev(pos+1)) is incorrect: the second reversal has even length and does not guarantee the element reaches the front.

The algorithm never checks the necessary parity condition (a_i % 2 == i % 2) for the existence of a solution. It may attempt to sort an impossible permutation and only later output -1, after having performed illegal operations.
There is no guard to keep the number of performed reversals within the required limit of 5n/2. The algorithm can easily exceed this bound for larger n.

fixes.code:
import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    # Check parity condition (1-indexed)
    for i in range(n):
        if (a[i] % 2) != ((i+1) % 2):
            print(-1)
            return
    
    ops = []
    
    def rev(length):
        ops.append(length)
        a[:length] = a[:length][::-1]
    
    for end in range(n, 2, -2):
        # Place end at position end, end-1 at position end-1
        for val in [end, end-1]:
            pos = a.index(val)  # 0-indexed
            if pos == 0:
                pass
            elif pos % 2 == 0:  # pos+1 is odd (1-indexed odd)
                rev(pos + 1)
            else:  # pos is odd (0-indexed), pos+1 is even - need extra step
                rev(pos)      # pos is odd (0-indexed) so length=pos is odd
                rev(pos + 1)  # now val is at index 1, reverse pos+1... 
                # After rev(pos): a[0..pos-1] reversed, val was at pos, still at pos
                # Hmm, need to reconsider
                pass
        # After bringing val to front, reverse prefix of length `end` or `end-1`
    
    print(len(ops))
    print(*ops)

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()

import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    
    # Check parity condition (1-indexed)
    for i in range(n):
        if (a[i] % 2) != ((i+1) % 2):
            print(-1)
            return
    
    ops = []
    
    def rev(length):
        ops.append(length)
        a[:length] = a[:length][::-1]
    
    for end in range(n, 2, -2):
        # Place end at position end, end-1 at position end-1
        for val in [end, end-1]:
            pos = a.index(val)  # 0-indexed
            if pos == 0:
                pass
            elif pos % 2 == 0:  # pos+1 is odd (1-indexed odd)
                rev(pos + 1)
            else:  # pos is odd (0-indexed), pos+1 is even - need extra step
                rev(pos)      # pos is odd (0-indexed) so length=pos is odd
                rev(pos + 1)  # now val is at index 1, reverse pos+1... 
                # After rev(pos): a[0..pos-1] reversed, val was at pos, still at pos
                # Hmm, need to reconsider
                pass
        # After bringing val to front, reverse prefix of length `end` or `end-1`
    
    print(len(ops))
    print(*ops)

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()

import sys
input = sys.stdin.readline

def solve():
    n = int(input())
    a = list(map(int, input().split()))
    ops = []
    
    def rev(p):
        a[:p] = a[:p][::-1]
        ops.append(p)
    
    end = n - 1  # 0-indexed, current last position to fill
    
    while end >= 2:
        # Place a[end] = end+1, a[end-1] = end
        # First place end+1 at position end
        v = end + 1
        i = a.index(v)
        if i == end:
            pass  # already placed
        else:
            if i != 0:
                if i % 2 == 0:  # i is even (0-based), i+1 is odd
                    rev(i + 1)
                else:  # i is odd (0-based), i+1 is even - can't reverse directly
                    # reverse prefix of length i (odd since i is odd)
                    rev(i)
                    # now v is at position i, which is odd (0-based)... wait
                    # after reversing prefix of length i, a[0..i-1] reversed
                    # v was at index i, still at index i
                    # Hmm that doesn't help
                    # Try: reverse prefix of length 1 first? no-op
                    # Actually reverse prefix i: a[i] is still v
                    # Now i is odd, so i+1 is even... 
                    # Let's try a different approach: 
                    # reverse prefix of length i+2 if i+2 <= end+1 and i+2 is odd?
                    # i is odd, i+2 is odd! 
                    if i + 2 <= end + 1:
                        # undo the rev(i) we just did
                        # Actually let's reconsider without doing rev(i) first
                        pass
                    # Let me redo this logic
            if i == 0:
                rev(end + 1)
            else:
                i = a.index(v)
                if i % 2 == 0:  # 0-based even -> 1-based odd
                    rev(i + 1)
                    rev(end + 1)
                else:  # 0-based odd -> 1-based even
                    # Use 3 ops: bring neighbor to front first
                    rev(i + 2) if (i + 2) % 2 == 1 else None
                    # i+2 is odd since i is odd
                    rev(i + 2)
                    rev(end + 1)
        
        # Now place end at position end-1
        v2 = end
        j = a.index(v2)
        if j == end - 1:
            pass
        else:
            if j == 0:
                rev(end)  # end is odd since end was n-1 and n is odd, so end is even... 
                # wait n is odd, n-1 is even, end starts at n-1 (even)
                # end is even (0-based index), end as prefix length = end (even) - not valid!
                pass
            else:
                pass
        
        end -= 2
    
    # Check if sorted
    if a == list(range(1, n+1)):
        print(len(ops))
        print(*ops)
    else:
        print(-1)

t = int(input())
for _ in range(t):
    solve()
